one_line_takeaway: A cognitive-layer-focused literature survey (not a novel attack/defense) arguing that LLM-controlled robotics needs security taxonomies distinct from text-only LLM security because attacks there must produce physically executable, context-dependently-harmful action policies, and that current defenses remain fragmented across perception/planning/control layers with no unified cross-layer assurance.
key_finding: The survey's central synthesized claim is that LLM-controlled robotics introduces a 'text-to-action shift' that breaks the assumptions of text-only LLM safety research: harmfulness becomes state/context-dependent (e.g., 'move forward' is safe in open space but catastrophic near a cliff) rather than universally flaggable, and a successful attack must yield not just harmful semantic content but a syntactically valid, physically executable action policy compatible with the robot's API (termed 'policy executability', drawn from cited work on ROBOPAIR/POEX). The survey further concludes that current defenses are fragmented and siloed by layer: formal/logic-based specifications (e.g., Safety Chip, Safety Guardrails) don't extend to continuous robot dynamics; control-theoretic safeguards (e.g., CBFs) are decoupled from LLM-level reasoning; multi-LLM oversight relies on shared model-family alignment and offers no adversarial-robustness guarantee; and evaluation/compliance frameworks rely on static metrics in controlled simulation rather than long-horizon, multimodal, real-deployment uncertainty -- i.e., no end-to-end, cross-layer assurance currently exists.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an original attack/defense/empirical paper). Its own contribution is a systematic literature review and taxonomy specifically for the security of LLM-controlled robotics at the 'cognitive layer' (the LLM planning/reasoning module), which the authors argue is a gap left unaddressed by prior surveys that either cover general LLM security (purely digital/textual harms) or LLM-robotics integration (operational reliability, not adversarial threats). The paper's own contributions are: (1) a threat model organizing attacker capability into white-box/gray-box/black-box tiers adapted to the LLM-robot architecture (planning, translation, execution, feedback-loop components); (2) an attack taxonomy across three families -- Jailbreaking, Backdoor, and Prompt Injection -- each broken into sub-mechanisms, synthesizing findings from a large body of cited work; (3) a defense taxonomy structured along three axes (safety lifecycle: prevent/detect/mitigate/recover/assure; system layer: perception, planning, execution, world-model, interfaces, infrastructure; mechanism family, e.g., formal specification, multi-LLM oversight, prompt hardening, state/memory management, runtime rule-based filters, control-theoretic safety, contextual grounding, safe plan synthesis, testing/metrics, standards compliance), with a comparative table (Table II) mapping six representative defense papers to these axes; (4) a catalogue of datasets/benchmarks for LLM-robotics security evaluation, organized into attack-specific benchmarks, general-purpose simulation environments, and code-generating frameworks (Table III); (5) a comparative table (Table I) positioning this survey against six other related surveys; and (6) a discussion section arguing that traditional (text-only) LLM defenses are insufficient for embodied robotics because of the 'text-to-action shift' and context-dependent harmfulness, plus a research agenda highlighting under-explored areas (multimodal LLM security, cross-layer defense integration).
threat_model: The survey's own threat model (Section III.B) categorizes attacker capability into three tiers applied to the four-component LLM-robot control loop (Planning/'Brain', Translation, Execution, Feedback): White-box (full knowledge of LLM weights/architecture and robot API/system parameters -- least practical, simulates insider/full leakage), Gray-box (knows which LLM and its planning API but lacks access to low-level motor controllers or hardware safety filters -- described as the most realistic and dangerous middle ground), and Black-box/no-box (no internal knowledge, interacts only via external voice/text input -- most practical but relies on trial-and-error). This is the paper's own organizing framework for classifying the attacks and defenses it surveys, not a threat model tested empirically by the authors.
limitations: As a survey, its main limitations are inherent to the genre: no new empirical validation, no PRISMA-style transparency on total papers screened/included/excluded, and reliance on the accuracy/completeness of the underlying cited studies. The paper itself acknowledges (Table I, 'Limitations' column) that its own scope is confined to the 'cognitive layer' (LLM planning/reasoning) and explicitly excludes attacks on low-level perception, vision, or motor-control modules, and that defenses surveyed remain fragmented across the safety lifecycle -- gaps the authors call out as an open research agenda rather than something they solved (e.g., formal specs vs. continuous dynamics gap, CBFs decoupled from LLM reasoning, ICL-backdoor sustainability under long-context dilution unresolved, ASR-vs-utility trade-off in backdoor defenses unresolved).

datasets_used: None produced by this paper -- it is a survey. Datasets/benchmarks it catalogues from cited work (Table III): AGENTSAFE (custom simulation, hazardous-instruction refusal), SafeAgentBench (AI2-THOR, 10 harm categories plus safe-task control group), ASIMOV Benchmark (multimodal dataset, 'robot constitutions' semantic-safety/value alignment), Harmful-RLbench (custom simulation, policy-executable jailbreak from the POEX paper), EIRAD (AI2-THOR, decision-level robustness dataset from Liu et al.), CALVIN (custom simulation, language-conditioned long-horizon manipulation), RLBench (V-REP, 100 manipulation tasks), VIMA (PyBullet, multimodal-prompt manipulation), VLABench (MuJoCo, long-horizon VLA evaluation), DROID (real-world Franka Panda), RoboCasa (MuJoCo, 100+ kitchen tasks), VirtualHome (Unity3D, program-based household activities)
quantitative_results: All numbers below are results the survey reports as findings of the specific cited papers, not results generated by this survey's own authors.
Safety Guardrails [97] (cited work): cuts unsafe plan executions from over 90% to under 2.5%

'Enhancing Reliability' [89] (cited work): reports a 30.8% increase in task success rate under injection attacks, and up to 325% increase in complex scenarios involving ambiguous or multi-modal instructions
SafeEmbodAI [98] (cited work): introduces the Mission-Oriented Exploration Rate (MOER) metric, reporting 267% gains under adversarial conditions
SafeAgentBench (cited work, cataloged not re-evaluated): 10 distinct harm categories (e.g. fire hazards, electrical shocks) plus a safe-task control group
RoboCasa (cited work, cataloged): 100+ tasks with procedural kitchen-scene generation
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the traditional sense -- this survey performs no experiments and compares no baselines itself. It instead comparatively tables six prior related surveys against its own scope (Table I: Kim et al. 2024 'Integration of LLMs with Intelligent Robots'; Zeng et al. 'LLMs for Robotics: A Survey'; Li et al. 2025 'LLMs for Multi-Robot Systems'; 'The Emerged Security and Privacy of LLM Agent'; Xing et al. 'Towards Robust and Secure Embodied AI'; 'Evaluating Security Risks in Robotics Powered by LLMs'), and six representative defense frameworks against lifecycle/layer/mechanism-family axes (Table II: Safety Guardrails for LLM-Enabled Robots, Plug in the Safety Chip, SafeEmbodAI, Enhancing Reliability, Safety-Aware Task Planning via LLMs (SAFER), CEE).
